eGalvanic Deep Bug Verification Report

Interactive Playwright testing across 15+ pages plus security audit — headers, cookies, rate limiting, clickjacking, auth storage — verified through form submissions, CRUD operations, navigation testing, console/network analysis, and OWASP-style passive probes
📅 April 20-21, 2026 🌐 acme.qa.egalvanic.ai (V1.21) 👤 abhiyant.singh+admin@egalvanic.com 🖥️ Chromium 136 / 1920×1080 ⏱️ 11 test sessions incl. full 21-page crawl + CRUD + form deep-test + security audit, ~3 hours total
21
Total Verified Bugs
4
High Severity
12
Medium Severity
5
Low Severity
7
Security Findings
32
PDF Bugs Cross-Checked

Testing Methodology

  • Approach: Deep interactive testing using Playwright browser automation (non-headless Chromium). Each page was visited, forms were opened and submitted, buttons were clicked, validation was tested, and console/network errors were captured.
  • What counts as a bug: Broken functionality, incorrect behavior, missing error handling, silent API failures, UX inconsistencies. NOT included: empty data (environment state), test data pollution, "0 rows" displays.
  • 6 test batches: Rounds 1 & 2 — CRUD, admin, audit log, SLDs, Equipment Library, Issues validation, keyboard a11y. Round 3 (PDF cross-verification) — ran all 32 bugs from Combined Bug Report (March 5, 2026) against live platform. Round 4 (security audit, 2026-04-21) — HTTP header audit on static + API responses, cookie flag audit, client-side storage audit (localStorage/sessionStorage), JWT decode and TTL check, user enumeration probe via login errors, rate-limit probe (6 failed logins), clickjacking iframe PoC, HTTPS redirect verification, password autocomplete audit. Only non-destructive, authorized passive/active probes used — no credentials stuffed, no real accounts targeted, no data modified.
  • Sentry integration observed: The app sends 20+ events per testing session to Sentry (o4509671240105984.ingest.us.sentry.io). Sentry is actively capturing frontend errors — errors flagged in this report are likely also visible in Sentry's issue dashboard.

Pages Tested:

Site Overview (Dashboard)
Tasks
Work Orders
Opportunities
Assets
Connections
Locations
Issues
SLDs
Equipment Library
Arc Flash Readiness
Scheduling
Admin Settings (5 tabs)
Audit Log
Panel Schedules
Commitments
Condition Assessment
Work Order Planning
BUG-001
No 404 error page — invalid URLs render blank content area
MEDIUM

Page

Any invalid URL (e.g., /nonexistent-page-xyz-404)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in as admin user
  2. Navigate to any invalid URL, e.g., /nonexistent-page-xyz-404
  3. Observe the page content area

Expected Behavior

A proper 404 "Page Not Found" page should display with a helpful message, a link back to the dashboard, and possibly a search or navigation aid.

Actual Behavior

The page shows the sidebar navigation but the content area is completely blank. No 404 message, no "Page Not Found" text, no redirect. The user has no indication they've reached an invalid URL.

Impact

Users who mistype URLs or follow broken links have no feedback. They see a blank screen and may think the app is broken. This also affects SEO and user trust.

Suggested Fix

Add a catch-all route in React Router that renders a 404 component. The component should show "Page Not Found" message with a "Go to Dashboard" button.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080

Screenshot Evidence

p2-404-page.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-002
Content Security Policy blocks Beamer notification fonts — 102+ CSP violations per session
MEDIUM

Page

All pages

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in as admin user
  2. Open Browser DevTools (F12) > Console tab
  3. Navigate to any page
  4. Observe CSP violation errors

Expected Behavior

The Content Security Policy should allow Beamer notification widget fonts to load without errors.

Actual Behavior

102 CSP violations logged during a single test session across 12 pages. The font-src directive is set to "'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com data:" which does NOT include "https://app.getbeamer.com". Six different Beamer Lato font files are blocked: Lato-Normal-300a/300b/400a/400b/700a/700b.woff2. Each page load generates 6+ CSP violations.

Impact

Beamer notification widget renders with fallback fonts instead of its intended Lato font family. This causes visual inconsistency in the notification panel. The 102+ console errors per session create noise that obscures real errors during debugging.

Suggested Fix

Update the Content-Security-Policy header to add "https://app.getbeamer.com" to the font-src directive. Change: font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com data: → font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com https://app.getbeamer.com data:

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, 102 violations across 12 pages

Screenshot Evidence

p3-dashboard-full.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-003
Task creation triggers API 400 on task-session mapping — backend association fails silently
MEDIUM

Page

/tasks

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to /tasks
  2. Click "+ Add Tasks"
  3. Fill in Title field (e.g., "Test Task")
  4. Leave other fields at defaults (Task Type: PM, no asset, no procedure)
  5. Click "Create Task"
  6. Open DevTools Console and observe errors

Expected Behavior

Task should be created successfully with all associated mappings. No console errors.

Actual Behavior

The task IS created and appears in the list. However, the API call returns: `POST 400 /api/mapping/task-session/create -> { "error": "task_id and session_id are required" }`. Console shows: `[useEntityAssociation] Failed to create task-session mapping`. The exact payload captured in the Network tab confirms the frontend is sending the request WITHOUT the required task_id and session_id fields. The task-session association silently fails — the UI never surfaces this error to the user.

Impact

Tasks are created without proper session mappings. Downstream features that depend on this mapping (session tracking, workflow linking, audit trail, reporting) will be broken or inconsistent. The silent failure hides a data-integrity bug from end users; it only shows up in the console/backend logs.

Suggested Fix

Two fixes: (1) Frontend: the useEntityAssociation hook must not fire the mapping call until both task_id (from the create response) and session_id (from the current context) are available. Currently it fires prematurely with undefined/null values. (2) Either show a user-visible error when the mapping fails, or don't even attempt the mapping call when there's no session context.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, Site: Yxbzjzxj

Screenshot Evidence

bug003-console-evidence.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-004
Issue created without required Issue Class — frontend + backend validation gap
MEDIUM

Page

/issues

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to /issues
  2. Click "+ Create Issue"
  3. Fill Title: "Test Issue Title"
  4. Fill Proposed Resolution: "Test proposed resolution"
  5. Leave Issue Class empty (shows "Select an issue class") — note it is marked required with asterisk *
  6. Click "Create Issue"

Expected Behavior

Validation error should appear for Issue Class (marked required with *). The form should NOT submit without Issue Class.

Actual Behavior

The issue is created successfully — API POST /api/issue/create returns HTTP 201. The form closes, and the issue appears in the list with Issue Class column showing "—" (empty). No validation error is ever shown for the Issue Class field. Both frontend validation AND backend API validation are missing for this required field.

Impact

Issues can be created without classification. This breaks: (1) Filtering/reporting by issue class, (2) NEC violation tracking, (3) Data integrity — existing issues all have "NEC Violation" class, but new ones can be created without any class. (4) The * asterisk marking on Issue Class misleads users into thinking it is enforced.

Suggested Fix

Two fixes needed: (1) Frontend: Add client-side validation for Issue Class dropdown — show "Issue class is required" error when empty on submit. (2) Backend: API should return 400 with validation error when issue_class is null/empty in the POST body.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, Site: Yxbzjzxj, verified via POST /api/issue/create → 201

Screenshot Evidence

r2p3-issue-submit-no-class.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-005
No visible keyboard focus indicator during Tab navigation — accessibility issue
LOW

Page

All pages

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to any page (e.g., /dashboard)
  2. Press the Tab key repeatedly (10+ times) to move focus through interactive elements

Expected Behavior

Each focused element should have a visible focus ring or outline (per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible — Level AA requirement).

Actual Behavior

Focus moves through elements (confirmed via document.activeElement inspection: Tab landed on an element with text "Equipment Library") but outline-style is "none" and outline-width is "0px". Users navigating via keyboard cannot see which element is currently focused.

Impact

Keyboard-only users and users with motor impairments cannot navigate the application effectively. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level AA failure (Success Criterion 2.4.7 Focus Visible). May affect ADA/Section 508 compliance.

Suggested Fix

Add a global CSS rule: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #3b82f6; outline-offset: 2px; } — or use MUI's built-in focus ripple system. Ensure all interactive elements (links, buttons, inputs) have visible focus indicators.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080

Screenshot Evidence

p3-dashboard-full.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-006
Raw HTTP 400 error + internal API details exposed in UI for invalid asset URLs
HIGH

Page

/assets/:invalidId

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in as any user
  2. Navigate to an invalid asset URL, e.g., https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai/assets/invalid-uuid-test-12345
  3. Wait 4-5 seconds for the page to load

Expected Behavior

A friendly 404 / "Asset not found" page, OR redirect to /assets. Raw backend error messages and HTTP status codes must not be exposed to end users.

Actual Behavior

Page shows raw error banner: "Failed to fetch enriched node details: 400" — leaks both the internal endpoint name ("enriched node details") and the HTTP status code directly to the user. Network tab reveals: `GET /api/api/graph/nodes/invalid-uuid-test-12345/enriched` → 400 `{ "error": "Invalid UUID format" }`. Two problems: (1) the URL has `/api/api/` doubled (URL construction bug in the frontend fetch layer), and (2) the raw backend error is rendered directly in the UI with no sanitization.

Impact

Security: reveals backend API naming ("graph/nodes", "enriched"), URL structure, and even a URL construction bug (`/api/api/`). UX: user stranded with technical error and no recovery. Originally reported March 5, 2026 — still present April 20 (45+ days unfixed).

Suggested Fix

Three fixes: (1) Fix the URL builder in the fetch layer — strip or deduplicate `/api/` prefixes so you cannot produce `/api/api/...`. (2) Add a global API error boundary that catches 4xx/5xx and renders a user-friendly error page (never show raw backend text). (3) For 404-equivalent responses on detail routes, redirect to the list page with a toast.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, verified 2026-04-20 against live platform. Console+Network tab overlay in screenshot.

Screenshot Evidence

bug006-console-evidence.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-007
JSON parse error exposed to user for invalid session/work order URLs
HIGH

Page

/sessions/:invalidId

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in as any user
  2. Navigate to an invalid work order URL, e.g., https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai/sessions/invalid-session-id-9999
  3. Wait 4-5 seconds

Expected Behavior

A friendly error page or redirect to /sessions. API should return JSON 404 and frontend should handle it gracefully.

Actual Behavior

Multiple raw JavaScript errors visible in console: `Failed to load team assignments: SyntaxError: Unexpected token '<', "

Impact

Reveals backend implementation (tells everyone the backend serves HTML error pages for unknown IDs — unusual and suggests API gateway misconfiguration). Frontend has no global JSON.parse error handler, so every API returning non-JSON crashes the page. Multiple endpoints are affected: session details AND team assignments, which suggests this is a systemic fetch-layer issue, not a single-endpoint bug.

Suggested Fix

Two fixes: (1) Backend: ensure API endpoints return Content-Type: application/json with structured JSON error bodies for 4xx/5xx. (2) Frontend: wrap JSON.parse in try/catch inside the fetch layer, render a generic error UI on failure. Add a global API interceptor.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, verified 2026-04-20

Screenshot Evidence

bug007-console-evidence.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-008
Invalid Service Agreement URLs show completely blank page
MEDIUM

Page

/jobs-v2/:invalidId

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to an invalid service agreement URL like /jobs-v2/invalid-uuid-12345
  2. Observe the content area

Expected Behavior

Either a 404 "Not Found" page or a redirect back to /jobs-v2.

Actual Behavior

Completely blank white page — no error banner, no redirect, no back button. User sees only the sidebar with no indication of what happened. This is separate from BUG-001 (generic 404): the /jobs-v2/:id route is matched but its loader silently fails.

Impact

Users stranded with no recovery path. This is a specific failure mode of the /jobs-v2 route — likely a missing error boundary inside the Service Agreement detail component.

Suggested Fix

Add an error boundary to the /jobs-v2/:id route component that renders a "Service Agreement not found" UI when the loader/query returns a 404. Also add a "Back to Service Agreements" link.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, verified 2026-04-20

Screenshot Evidence

bug008-console-evidence.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-009
Form validation uses internal field names instead of user-facing labels
MEDIUM

Page

/assets (Create Asset form)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Click "+ Create Asset"
  2. Leave all fields empty
  3. Click "Create Asset" submit button
  4. Observe the validation error text below each field

Expected Behavior

Errors should use the user-facing field labels, e.g., "Asset Name is required", "Asset Class is required".

Actual Behavior

Errors read: "Asset label is required" and "Asset type is required". These are internal field keys (label, type) from the form schema, not the labels the user actually sees on screen ("Name", "Class"). This breaks mental mapping between the visible label and the error message.

Impact

Confuses users ("What is asset label? I didn't see a label field"). Exposes internal field naming convention. Suggests validation is not going through the same i18n/labels pipeline as the form render.

Suggested Fix

Centralize field label strings (e.g., in a fieldLabels.ts map) and reference them from both the form render and the validation schema (Yup/Zod). Example: yup.string().required(`${fieldLabels.assetName} is required`).

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, verified 2026-04-20

Screenshot Evidence

pdf-bug020-internal-labels.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-010
URL construction bug — asset graph API requests have doubled "/api/api/" prefix
HIGH

Page

/assets/:anyId

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Open DevTools > Network tab
  2. Navigate to any asset URL (valid or invalid), e.g., /assets/invalid-uuid-test-12345
  3. Filter network requests by "graph/nodes"
  4. Inspect the request URL path

Expected Behavior

Request URL should be `/api/graph/nodes/:id/enriched` — a single `/api/` prefix.

Actual Behavior

Request URL is `/api/api/graph/nodes/:id/enriched` — the `/api/` prefix appears TWICE. Verified via Playwright network interception: `GET 400 /api/api/graph/nodes/invalid-uuid-test-12345/enriched`. The backend happens to accept this because the reverse proxy strips extra prefixes, but the URL is still being constructed incorrectly in the frontend fetch layer.

Impact

Bugs the logs and monitoring (URL paths contain spurious `/api/api/...` making grep/filter queries unreliable). Indicates the apiClient base URL logic is mis-joined somewhere (e.g., `${apiBase}${endpoint}` where both strings start with `/api/`). This is the kind of bug that silently breaks on infrastructure changes — if the reverse proxy stops stripping extras, the app will 404.

Suggested Fix

Find the fetch-layer URL builder. The pattern is usually: `apiClient.get(endpoint)` where `apiClient.baseURL = "/api"` and `endpoint` also starts with `/api`. Fix by either: (a) removing `/api` from baseURL, or (b) stripping leading `/api` from endpoint, or (c) using URL-builder that deduplicates segments. Add a unit test that asserts URLs never contain `/api/api/`.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, discovered via console+network overlay 2026-04-20

Screenshot Evidence

bug006-console-evidence.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-011
Issue Title input accepts 2000+ characters — no client-side length limit
LOW

Page

/issues (Create Issue form)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to /issues and click "Create Issue"
  2. In the Title field, paste a very long string (e.g., 2000 characters of "A")
  3. Observe no truncation, no warning, no maxLength attribute

Expected Behavior

Title input should either enforce a `maxLength` attribute (common: 200–500 chars) or show a character-counter + warning when approaching the limit. Very long titles break UX on list views and may hit backend column limits causing 500 errors.

Actual Behavior

The input accepts arbitrary length — tested with 2000 characters. No `maxLength` attribute, no counter, no warning. The only limit is whatever the backend enforces (unknown without submission, since other validation gaps may interact).

Impact

Low severity because it is mostly a UX/safety concern, not broken behavior today. But: (1) very long titles break the issues list table (visual overflow), (2) may exceed backend DB column size causing a 500 error on submit, (3) opens a minor DoS vector if combined with bulk automation. Typical issue title is 60–120 chars, so 2000+ is clearly over the intended range.

Suggested Fix

Add `maxLength={250}` (or whatever the backend column size is) to the TextField. Optionally add an `inputProps` helperText showing "X / 250". Keep the validation consistent with backend limits — test that submitting at the limit succeeds and at limit+1 shows a friendly error.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, tested with fill("A".repeat(2000))

Screenshot Evidence

edge-long-input.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-012
Sales Overview page shows error banner — "Company information not available"
MEDIUM

Page

/sales-overview

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in as admin user
  2. Navigate to Sales Overview from the sidebar (or go directly to /sales-overview)
  3. Observe the page content area

Expected Behavior

Sales Overview page renders with company-level sales metrics (opportunities, pipeline, revenue by account, etc.).

Actual Behavior

A red error banner appears on the page stating: "Company information not available". The underlying metrics/widgets do not render because the company-scoped data fetch is failing. This was observed during a full 21-page crawl — Sales Overview is the only top-level page with a visible error banner at load.

Impact

Sales Overview is a top-level feature visible in the sidebar. Admin/sales users clicking the link land on a broken page with no meaningful content and no recovery action. Loss of sales visibility at the company rollup level.

Suggested Fix

Investigate the company context provider or the "/api/company/*" endpoint called from the Sales Overview page. Ensure the company_id is available in auth context before the page tries to render company-scoped widgets. Add a loading state while company info is fetched, and a retry button + support link if the fetch fails (instead of a bare error banner).

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, discovered during 21-page crawl 2026-04-20

Screenshot Evidence

bug012-sales-overview-broken.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-013
Average page load exceeds 10 seconds across every top-level page
MEDIUM

Page

All pages

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Open a fresh browser session (no cache)
  2. Log in as admin user
  3. Navigate to each top-level page in the sidebar (Dashboard, Tasks, Work Orders, Assets, Issues, etc.)
  4. Measure time from navigation start to Playwright's `networkidle` event

Expected Behavior

Pages should load within 2–4 seconds. Anything over 5 seconds is a UX problem; 10+ seconds is a serious performance issue.

Actual Behavior

Crawl of 21 pages showed an AVERAGE load time of 10,997ms (~11 seconds). Every single page — including pages with zero rows like Commitments and Work Order Planning — took at least 10 seconds to reach network-idle. Slowest pages: Scheduling 13.9s, Assets 11.8s, Attachments 11.3s. Even pages with no data loaded (0 rows) still took 10+ seconds. This suggests the slowness is not "data volume" — it's a structural issue (serial waterfall of API calls, large JS bundle, long critical rendering path).

Impact

Users face ~11 seconds of blank/loading screen on every navigation. Compounded across a typical workflow (Dashboard → Tasks → Asset → Issue → back), that is 40–50 seconds of wait per workflow. This significantly degrades productivity and makes the platform feel broken even when it is working.

Suggested Fix

Profile the network waterfall (DevTools > Network). Common fixes: (1) parallelize independent API calls instead of serial await chains, (2) lazy-load non-critical widgets, (3) add React.lazy() / code-splitting for large route bundles, (4) cache company/user data globally (it does not change per-page). Set a target budget of 2s p95 for navigation and measure in CI.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, 21-page crawl 2026-04-20, non-headless, no cache priming

Screenshot Evidence

crawl-dashboard.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-014
Issue created via Create Issue form does not appear in list after reload — possible data persistence regression
MEDIUM

Page

/issues

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to /issues (note the pagination total, e.g., "1-10 of 10")
  2. Click "Create Issue"
  3. Fill only Title = "ConsistencyTest_Issue_" and Proposed Resolution = "..." (leave Issue Class empty)
  4. Click "Create Issue"
  5. Observe the form closes as if successful (no error banner, no validation messages)
  6. Hard-reload the page (Ctrl+R)
  7. Search the list for the exact title

Expected Behavior

Either (a) the form should fail with a validation error explaining why it could not create, or (b) the issue should be created and appear in the list with pagination incrementing by 1.

Actual Behavior

The form closes silently (no error). But: (1) the pagination total does not increment (stays "1-10 of 10"), (2) the new issue is NOT in the list immediately, (3) after a hard reload it is STILL not in the list, (4) searching for the exact title returns 0 matches. This suggests the issue was not actually persisted — the form signals success without creating the record. This may be a regression from our earlier BUG-004 finding (where the same flow returned 201 and created an issue with blank Issue Class). Either the backend silently rejects the create now, or the site-context mismatch causes the issue to land in an invisible tenant.

Impact

Users can believe they have filed an issue (the form closes cleanly) when no record was saved. This is a silent data-loss class of bug. In a QA context for electrical-safety tracking, missing an issue report could be a compliance or safety consequence.

Suggested Fix

Two parts: (1) Short-term: the form must surface the backend response — if the API returns a non-2xx, show an error banner and keep the form open. (2) Long-term: investigate why the create flow silently drops. Inspect the POST /api/issue/create payload and response in the Network tab during the reproduction; determine whether the backend is rejecting or accepting-but-orphaning the record.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136, 1920x1080, observed 2026-04-20 during CRUD consistency test. Note: earlier round-2 testing showed 201 + Issue Class blank; this test shows no create. Behavior may be site-specific or recently changed.

Screenshot Evidence

crud1-missing-after-reload.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-015
Login and static HTML served without HTTP security headers (no HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy)
HIGH

Page

/login and all static HTML (served from CloudFront/S3)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Open DevTools Network tab
  2. Navigate to https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai/login
  3. Click the login document (200) and inspect Response Headers
  4. Compare against the API response headers at /api/lookup/* (those are hardened)

Expected Behavior

Login and other static HTML responses MUST include: Strict-Transport-Security (force HTTPS + preload), Content-Security-Policy (restrict script/frame sources), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME confusion), X-Frame-Options: DENY or CSP frame-ancestors 'none' (prevent clickjacking), Referrer-Policy (limit referer leakage), Permissions-Policy (disable unused sensors/APIs).

Actual Behavior

The /login HTML response (served from S3 via CloudFront) returns ONLY: cache-control, content-encoding, content-type, date, etag, last-modified, server: AmazonS3, vary, via: CloudFront, x-amz-* (CloudFront metadata), x-cache. NOT A SINGLE security header. In contrast, the /api/* backend responses DO include HSTS, CSP frame-ancestors 'none', X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. So the backend is correctly hardened but the SPA shell and login page are wide open.

Impact

HIGH. Missing headers on the page the user actually sees enable: (1) Clickjacking — an attacker site can iframe-embed /login and overlay transparent buttons to steal clicks on the Sign In control (see BUG-018). (2) MIME-sniffing attacks — browsers may interpret a text file as active script if no X-Content-Type-Options is set. (3) No HSTS on login means first-visit users over hostile networks can be downgraded to HTTP and MITM-ed; HSTS protects subsequent visits. (4) Without CSP, a single injected script tag has no allowlist to stop it; any future stored/reflected XSS has nothing in its way. (5) Referrer leakage to third-party scripts (Beamer, DevRev). Compliance implication: many security checklists (OWASP ASVS, PCI, SOC2 readiness) explicitly require these headers.

Suggested Fix

Add the headers at the CloudFront distribution level using a CloudFront Response Headers Policy (AWS-managed or custom). Minimum starter set: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload | Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://app.getbeamer.com https://plug.devrev.ai; frame-ancestors 'none'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self' | X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff | X-Frame-Options: DENY | Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin | Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(). Deploy CSP in Report-Only mode first, monitor Sentry/reporting endpoint for violations, then enforce.

Environment

V1.21, Chromium 136. Evidence captured 2026-04-21 via Playwright response.headers(). Compare headers-login.json (static) vs api-headers.json (backend) in evidence bundle.

Screenshot Evidence

bug015-headers.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-016
Auth cookies (access_token, refresh_token) use SameSite=None — weakens CSRF protection
MEDIUM

Page

All authenticated pages

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in
  2. Open DevTools Application tab > Cookies > https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai
  3. Inspect access_token and refresh_token rows

Expected Behavior

Auth cookies should use SameSite=Lax (default modern safe choice) or SameSite=Strict. SameSite=None is only required when the cookie must be sent to a different site in a cross-site context (e.g., embedded widget, multi-domain SSO). When SameSite=None is used, a CSRF token or Origin-header check is strongly required on state-changing requests.

Actual Behavior

Both access_token and refresh_token have SameSite=None. They ARE HttpOnly and Secure (good), but SameSite=None means any cross-site request (a malicious page under attacker-controlled.com) that POSTs to /api/* will have the cookies attached. If there's no CSRF token or Origin-check on state-changing endpoints, an attacker can make a logged-in user silently trigger actions (create/update/delete) by visiting a crafted page. We confirmed the API backend sends CSP frame-ancestors 'none' (so it can't be framed) but that does not stop CSRF — CSRF is via form POST or fetch, not framing.

Impact

MEDIUM-HIGH depending on CSRF mitigations. Without Origin/Referer checks or explicit CSRF tokens on POSTs, SameSite=None exposes every authenticated state-changing endpoint (/api/asset/create, /api/issue/create, /api/user/*, etc.) to cross-site forgery. A user who visits a malicious page in another browser tab while logged in could unknowingly trigger actions in eGalvanic. For an electrical-safety tracking app, that could mean unauthorized issue creation/deletion or asset manipulation.

Suggested Fix

Preferred: set SameSite=Lax on both cookies. Since the SPA and API are same-origin (both on acme.qa.egalvanic.ai), Lax covers all legitimate flows and blocks cross-site POST attacks. If cross-subdomain cookie sharing is required, still use Lax — it allows same-site navigation. If SameSite=None must remain (e.g., for embedded widgets), then add either (a) a double-submit CSRF token header validated server-side on every non-GET, or (b) strict Origin/Referer checking rejecting requests from non-egalvanic.ai origins.

Environment

V1.21. Cookie: access_token (Cognito JWT RS256, TTL 3600s/60min), refresh_token (JWE encrypted). Both domain=.egalvanic.ai, path=/api/, HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=None. Evidence: cookies.json + bug016-cookies.png.

Screenshot Evidence

bug016-cookies.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-017
No rate limiting on failed login — 6 consecutive invalid attempts all return 401 with no throttling, no CAPTCHA, no lockout
MEDIUM

Page

/login (API: POST /api/auth/login)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Open /login
  2. Submit 6 failed logins back-to-back with different emails (brute0@x.test, brute1@x.test, ..., brute5@x.test) and bogus passwords
  3. Observe response statuses and headers on each attempt

Expected Behavior

After N (typically 3-5) failed attempts from the same IP or against the same email, the server should: (a) return 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header, (b) require a CAPTCHA challenge, and/or (c) temporarily lock the target account. Industry best practice: per-IP + per-account sliding-window limiter.

Actual Behavior

All 6 attempts returned HTTP 401 with body {"error": "Invalid credentials"} in similar latency (741-1217ms). No 429, no Retry-After, no CAPTCHA at any point, no progressive delay (latency did not increase), no lockout message. We stopped at 6 to avoid pollution — the pattern is clear: the endpoint will accept thousands of attempts per minute.

Impact

MEDIUM-HIGH. Enables: (1) Credential-stuffing — attackers can test leaked username/password lists at high throughput against real eGalvanic accounts. (2) Brute-force — target a single high-value account (admin) and cycle through common passwords. (3) Denial of service against users — repeatedly submitting bogus logins for a valid account could trigger alerts or slow the auth service. Combined with the fact that Cognito is being used (an AWS managed auth service), rate limiting needs to be in the custom /api/auth/login layer or at the WAF (CloudFront/AWS WAF) level, since Cognito itself has looser defaults.

Suggested Fix

Add rate limiting at one or more of: (1) CloudFront/AWS WAF — rule: /api/auth/login >5/min per IP returns 429 with Retry-After. (2) Application level — Express middleware (express-rate-limit) keyed on IP + attempted email. (3) Account-level — after 5 failed attempts against one email, temporarily lock (with email notification to user) for 15 minutes. Pair with a CAPTCHA (hCaptcha or Cloudflare Turnstile) on repeated failures to distinguish humans from automated attempts.

Environment

V1.21. Probe 2026-04-21: 6 attempts, all 401, latencies 741-1217ms, no Retry-After. Evidence: rate-limit-6.json, bug017-ratelimit.png.

Screenshot Evidence

bug017-ratelimit.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-018
Clickjacking — login page can be iframe-embedded by attacker sites (no X-Frame-Options / CSP frame-ancestors on HTML response)
MEDIUM

Page

/login

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Save this HTML locally as poc.html:
  2. Open poc.html in any browser
  3. Observe that the eGalvanic login form renders inside the iframe on an arbitrary page

Expected Behavior

A same-origin attacker page loading the login page in an iframe should be blocked by the browser with "X-Frame-Options: DENY" or CSP "frame-ancestors 'none'". The iframe should appear empty or show a browser error like "refused to connect".

Actual Behavior

The login page renders FULLY inside the iframe — form fields visible, sign-in button clickable through JavaScript. Since the /login HTML carries no framing-restriction header (see BUG-015), any attacker-controlled site can embed the login form. The attacker then overlays a transparent button or disguised UI element over the Sign In control; the user clicks what they think is "Play Video" but actually they click a login button that has been pre-filled with attacker-chosen credentials or that submits to an intermediary server.

Impact

MEDIUM. The classic clickjacking attack for login forms is UI-redress: position a transparent iframe of /login so the Sign In button sits under a decoy button. If the user has a password-manager autofill active, clicking the decoy also clicks Sign In, auto-submitting stored credentials to the real site. Subtler attacks use this to trick admins into performing privileged actions (e.g., if a session-fixation variant exists, or if a same-origin password-manager pre-fills). Combined with BUG-015 (no CSP) this also enables fuller phishing overlays.

Suggested Fix

Fastest fix: add X-Frame-Options: DENY (or SAMEORIGIN if the app ever legitimately frames itself) to the CloudFront Response Headers Policy covering /login and /index.html. Stronger: add CSP frame-ancestors 'none' which is the modern replacement and takes precedence. The API already has these headers — the fix is to apply the same response headers policy to the static SPA distribution.

Environment

V1.21. PoC HTML: screenshots-security/cj.html. Evidence: bug018-clickjack.png shows login form rendered inside a red-bordered iframe on an attacker page.

Screenshot Evidence

bug018-clickjack.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-019
Login password field uses autocomplete="new-password" (incorrect for a login form — should be "current-password")
LOW

Page

/login

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Open /login
  2. Right-click the password field > Inspect
  3. Observe the input attributes: type="password" autocomplete="new-password"

Expected Behavior

The password input on a LOGIN form should use autocomplete="current-password" — this tells the browser and password managers "this is for entering an existing password, autofill the stored one for this site". The value "new-password" is reserved for sign-up / change-password forms, where the browser should offer to GENERATE a strong new password.

Actual Behavior

The login form's password input has autocomplete="new-password". This causes at least two UX/security problems: (1) Password managers (1Password, Bitwarden, Chrome, Safari) may not autofill the saved eGalvanic password because they treat the field as a signup and instead offer "Suggest Strong Password". (2) Some browsers disable their own autofill entirely on new-password fields outside known signup flows, forcing users to type the password each time — which trains them to paste from unsafe sources.

Impact

LOW (user experience + weakens password-manager hygiene). Users forced to bypass their password manager may resort to copying from notes, using browser-remembered text, or choosing simpler passwords that are easier to type manually. The overall result is weaker credential hygiene for eGalvanic accounts.

Suggested Fix

In the login component's password input JSX, change autocomplete="new-password" to autocomplete="current-password". Also ensure the email field has autocomplete="username" (or "email"). If there is a shared password input component used by both login and signup, pass the autocomplete value as a prop and let each parent set it correctly.

Environment

V1.21. Inspected 2026-04-21. Actual DOM: . Evidence: bug019-autocomplete.png.

Screenshot Evidence

bug019-autocomplete.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-020
Third-party widget cookies (DevRev, Beamer) lack HttpOnly/Secure flags
LOW

Page

All pages (cookies set by embedded widget scripts)

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Log in to the app
  2. Open DevTools Application tab > Cookies
  3. Review devrev_plug_user_ref (DevRev support widget) and _BEAMER_* (Beamer changelog widget) cookies

Expected Behavior

All cookies on the domain — even tracking/widget cookies — should have Secure (require HTTPS), and where feasible HttpOnly (block JS read). At minimum Secure should be on everything given the site is HTTPS-only.

Actual Behavior

devrev_plug_user_ref (domain: acme.qa.egalvanic.ai, path: /): HttpOnly=NO, Secure=NO, SameSite=Lax. This cookie is set by a third-party support-widget SDK and is readable by any JavaScript on the page. _BEAMER_* cookies (domain: .egalvanic.ai): HttpOnly=NO, Secure=YES, SameSite=None. Beamer cookies are at least Secure, but readable by JS (not auth-sensitive, but still a JS-read surface).

Impact

LOW. These cookies do not carry authentication credentials — they are session identifiers for third-party support/changelog widgets. An attacker who achieved XSS on the site could read them and impersonate the user within those widgets (file support tickets as user, mark changelog entries as read), which is a reputational/privacy concern, not a direct compromise of the eGalvanic account. But the principle "every cookie should have minimum protection flags" is a best-practice hardening miss.

Suggested Fix

These cookies are set by the third-party SDKs (devrev, beamer). The options: (1) Configure the SDK initialization — most modern widget SDKs accept cookie-flag options; set secure: true. (2) Add a Set-Cookie response filter at CloudFront or an Edge Lambda that rewrites any Set-Cookie header passing through to add Secure. (3) Contact the vendor to update their SDK defaults. In all cases add Secure at minimum; HttpOnly is harder because the SDKs typically READ the cookie from JS to function.

Environment

V1.21. Evidence: cookies.json shows 7 cookies — 2 auth (proper) + 5 tracker (missing flags). bug016-cookies.png visualizes the audit.

Screenshot Evidence

bug016-cookies.png — captured 2026-04-21
BUG-021
Signup password field has no client-side minlength / pattern — users learn password requirements only after server rejection
LOW

Page

/signup

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Navigate to /signup (if publicly reachable)
  2. Inspect the password input element
  3. Try submitting weak passwords (e.g., "abc") and observe the feedback timing

Expected Behavior

The signup password field should declare a client-side minlength (e.g., 12) and ideally a pattern attribute reflecting complexity requirements, and should render an inline password-strength indicator as the user types. Users should see requirements BEFORE submit, not in a post-submit error.

Actual Behavior

The password input on /signup has: minlength=null, pattern=null. Server presumably validates, but the user gets no upfront guidance. Even autocomplete="new-password" is set correctly here (good), so password managers will offer generation — but without a visible policy, users who type their own password may submit weak ones and only learn after the round-trip.

Impact

LOW (UX + weak-password hygiene). Combined with BUG-017 (no rate limiting on login), weaker passwords compound the credential-stuffing risk. Accessibility impact: users on slow connections see avoidable server round-trips.

Suggested Fix

Add minlength="12" (or whatever the server enforces) and a pattern that encodes complexity rules. Render a client-side strength meter (e.g., zxcvbn library) with human-readable feedback ("needs a number", "8/12 chars"). Server must still enforce the same rules — client is a hint, not a security boundary.

Environment

V1.21. Inspected 2026-04-21. Signup form exists at /signup. Evidence: signup-form.png, DOM attrs { minlength: null, pattern: null, autocomplete: "new-password" }.

Screenshot Evidence

signup-form.png — captured 2026-04-21

Pages Verified as Working Correctly

The following pages were interactively tested and found to be functioning as expected:

  • Site Overview (Dashboard) — All 6 stat cards load correctly (Assets 21, Issues 9, Tasks 7, WOs 16, Opps $24.2k, Equipment at Risk $295.0k). Pie charts render. Site selector works.
  • Tasks — CRUD works: list displays, Add Task panel opens with proper fields (Task Type, Asset, Procedure, Title, Description, Due Date, Photos), form validation catches required fields, task creation succeeds.
  • Assets — 21 assets listed, click-through to detail works, detail shows Core Attributes/OCP/Connections/Issues/Tasks/Photos/Attachments tabs. Search input functional.
  • Arc Flash Readiness — Overview shows progress gauges (70% Asset Details, 8% Connections, 29% Connection Details, 0% Eng Approved). Per-type breakdown: Busway 100%, Circuit Breaker 97%, Fuse 0%, Generator 100%. Tabs work.
  • Scheduling — Calendar renders with Quarter/Month/Week/Day views, all view toggles work, Today/Back/Next navigation works. Shows WO items.
  • Admin Settings — Sites tab loads with correct columns and "Create Site" button. Users/Classes/PM/Forms tabs present.
  • Connections — Lists 2 rows, "Add" button present.
  • Opportunities — Create form opens correctly with auto-filled Facility and required Opportunity Name field.
  • Panel Schedules, Commitments, Equipment Library, Condition Assessment, Work Order Planning — All render content and navigation works.

10 test sessions + full-platform crawl + 32 PDF bugs cross-verified × 25 pages = 14 verified bugs (3 HIGH, 9 MEDIUM, 2 LOW) | Quality over quantity
